elements.sent-2411-164: The main goal of this note is to illustrate the content of \cite{MMP1 by explicit examples of {\bf what the solutions to simple Chalykh's equations for BAF's look like .}}
elements.sent-2411-165: We see that for $N>2$ these solutions are originally somewhat ugly, but get {\bf decomposed into nice factorized expressions .}
elements.sent-2411-166: These expression are somewhat difficult to generalize, moreover it is unclear if factorized decomposition is unique, and what kind of additional symmetry it reflects.
elements.sent-2411-167: It appears that important is the condition that extra factors depend only on variables associated to non-simple roots, though at the moment it is not clear how to put this in practice beyond the case of $N=3$ (where there is just one non-simple root, which delivers an extra degree of control).
elements.sent-2411-168: A complementary goal is to advertise the twisted BAF's: the claim is that they are not more complicated to work with than their usual non-twisted counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-169: At the same time, this twisted case is much more interesting for applications to the new sets of coexisting integrable systems \cite{MMP implied by the q,t-deformation \cite{Ch3 of the WLZZ models \cite{China,Ch12 and by the DIM algebra \cite{DIM .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-170: In this case, the Chalykh's equations are modified just slightly, but the solutions get far more involved and do not factorize immediately even at $N=2$.
elements.sent-2411-171: They are no longer related to the Macdonald polynomials, at least directly, which opens a set of questions about their meaning.
elements.sent-2411-172: Again, in this paper, we just raise and demonstrate the problem, postponing possible resolutions for the future.
elements.sent-2411-173: What we do, however, is provide additional evidence that {\bf the twisted BAF's are indeed the eigenfunctions of the Hamiltonians for non-trivial rays , at least for the integer rays.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Generalization to rational rays is still an open problem.
elements.sent-2411-175: Essentially, this whole text is about solutions of linear equations, i.e. a kind of Cramer's rule application to a simply looking system (\ref{linsys0 )+(\ref{Psideco ).}}
elements.sent-2411-176: It is just amazing to see how sophisticated the outcome is, and what a variety of structures emerge seemingly out of ``nothing''.
elements.sent-2411-177: It would be nice to find a better understanding of this phenomenon, and a better way to present it.
